APEX_THYROID_CATEGORIES.name: Primary Pituitary, Secondary Hypothyroidism
APEX_THYROID_CATEGORIES.summary: Pituitary-driven hypothyroidism (low TSH from CNS or stress drivers) plus at least one low downstream hormone.
options: CNS Serotonin deficiency → low TSH + serotonin-deficiency symptoms
CNS Dopamine deficiency → low TSH + dopamine-deficiency symptoms
Cytokine-promoted (IL-1, IL-6, IL-2, TNFα) — evaluate inflammation
Prolactin-promoted (low dopamine or progesterone deficiency)
Cortisol suppression — most commonly driven by dysglycemia

APEX_THYROID_CATEGORIES.name: Autoimmunity
APEX_THYROID_CATEGORIES.summary: Autoimmune attack on TPO or thyroglobulin (often presents with high TSH; if TSH is not high yet, autoimmunity may still be developing).
options: TPO autoimmune response (high TPO Ab) — Hashimoto's; can present hyperthyroid initially
Thyroglobulin autoimmune response (high Tgb Ab)
TSH-receptor autoimmune response (TSI/TR Ab) — causes hypo or hyper, Graves' disease; recommend TSI lab if hyperthyroid presentation
T3/T4 autoimmune response — uncommon; would require T3/T4 antibody labs

APEX_THYROID_CATEGORIES.name: Primary Hypothyroidism (non-autoimmune)
APEX_THYROID_CATEGORIES.summary: High TSH without elevated antibodies. May or may not have low downstream hormones. Commonly down-regulated TPO enzyme activity from progesterone or cofactor deficiencies. Note: monitor antibodies — may evolve into Cat 2 over time.
options: Progesterone deficiency (progesterone up-regulates TPO enzyme)
Cofactor deficiency (epinephrine/norepinephrine down-regulate TPO activity)

APEX_THYROID_CATEGORIES.name: Down-regulated 5-Deiodinase Activity (T4→T3 Conversion)
APEX_THYROID_CATEGORIES.summary: Normal T4 with low T3 — conversion issue. Drivers: cofactor deficiencies, GI dysbiosis, cytokines, cortisol, or peripheral serotonin/dopamine deficiencies. Up-regulation by testosterone or insulin can cause hormone resistance.
options: Cofactor deficiency
GI dysbiosis / lipopolysaccharides (~20% of T4→T3 conversion happens in the gut)
Elevated cytokines (inflammation)
Elevated cortisol
Peripheral serotonin deficiency
Peripheral dopamine deficiency
Up-regulated 5-deiodinase from elevated testosterone (insulin surges, testosterone, testosterone creams) → hormone resistance from too much T4→T3

APEX_THYROID_CATEGORIES.name: Normal Totals with Low Free Hormones
APEX_THYROID_CATEGORIES.summary: Total hormones within range but free fraction depressed — bioavailability issue at the binding/cellular level. Correlate with Cat 4 (conversion) and Cat 6 (binding).
options: Binding-globulin issue reducing free hormone fraction
Cellular uptake or membrane transport limitation
Evaluate alongside Cat 4 (5-deiodinase) if T3-related
Evaluate alongside Cat 6 (T3 Uptake) for binding-globulin involvement

APEX_THYROID_CATEGORIES.name: Receptor Site Resistance
APEX_THYROID_CATEGORIES.summary: Low T3 Uptake — binding/receptor issue at the tissue level. Resistance driven by inflammation, cortisol, homocysteine, or vitamin A deficiency. Common in autoimmune patients on replacement who still have thyroid symptoms.
options: Elevated TBG → decreased free-hormone fraction (commonly from estrogen, OCPs, pregnancy, HRT)
Resistance from elevated inflammation (typical in autoimmune patients on replacement)
Resistance from elevated cortisol
Resistance from vitamin A deficiency (thyroid hormone transcription requires retinoic acid receptors)
Resistance from elevated homocysteine (dampens thyroid hormone expression; raised by antacids, oral contraceptives, estrogen)

APEX_COMPUTED_PATTERNS.name: Inflammation
APEX_COMPUTED_PATTERNS.summary: Patterns consistent with active inflammation. Correlate with clinical symptoms and consider broader inflammatory workup.

APEX_COMPUTED_PATTERNS.name: Iron Overload
APEX_COMPUTED_PATTERNS.summary: Pattern consistent with iron overload. Consider hereditary hemochromatosis workup if levels are markedly elevated.

APEX_COMPUTED_PATTERNS.name: High Ferritin — Inflammation vs. Iron Overload (ambiguous)
APEX_COMPUTED_PATTERNS.summary: High ferritin with normal serum iron — could indicate either inflammation or early iron overload. Correlate with CRP, homocysteine, and clinical context.

APEX_COMPUTED_PATTERNS.name: Insulin Resistance / Dysglycemia
APEX_COMPUTED_PATTERNS.summary: Insulin resistance pattern. Supporting markers (when flagged) confirm the picture: elevated triglycerides — especially if ≥ cholesterol — depressed HDL, elevated LDL, elevated uric acid, elevated glucose.

APEX_COMPUTED_PATTERNS.name: Vitamin D Deficiency
APEX_COMPUTED_PATTERNS.summary: 25-hydroxy Vitamin D below functional range (≥50 ng/mL functional optimum). Consider supplementation to bring level into the 50–80 ng/mL range.

APEX_COMPUTED_PATTERNS.name: Possible NAFLD / MASLD (Early Stage)
APEX_COMPUTED_PATTERNS.summary: AST:ALT ratio < 1 (ALT dominant). Typical pattern in early-stage non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD / MASLD). ALT is usually the dominant enzyme in earlier stages.
options: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) — most common cause
Metabolic-associated steatotic liver disease (MASLD)
Insulin resistance / metabolic syndrome — correlate with glycemic markers
Hepatic steatosis from medications, weight, or diet

APEX_COMPUTED_PATTERNS.name: AST:ALT ≥ 2 — Alcoholic Liver Disease or Advanced Fibrosis
APEX_COMPUTED_PATTERNS.summary: AST:ALT ratio ≥ 2. Suggestive of alcoholic liver disease (AST rarely >500 in alcoholic hepatitis; ratio driven partly by pyridoxal-5-phosphate deficiency reducing ALT synthesis). In a patient with known NAFLD, this inversion often marks progression to advanced fibrosis or cirrhosis (incorporated into noninvasive scores like FIB-4).
options: Alcohol use (including underreported intake)
Advanced fibrosis or cirrhosis from underlying MASLD
Muscle source of AST (rhabdomyolysis, strenuous exercise, statin myopathy) — check CK
Hemolysis
Wilson's disease
Hemochromatosis
Autoimmune hepatitis with cirrhotic features

dysglycemia.name: Blood Sugar Handout
dysglycemia.intro: Reference handout for patients with insulin resistance, dysglycemia, or reactive hypoglycemia patterns.
